questions.prompt: In Chapter 1, the discussion about robots primarily functions as what kind of opening move by the author?
options: A wedge that turns ordinary talk into a question about what a human being is
A prediction that the plot will become science-fiction driven
A warning that technology will replace education
A metaphor for Sophie’s dislike of Joanna

questions.prompt: Sophie’s house ‘at the end of the world’ (edge of suburb, woods beyond) most strongly signals:
options: A psychological boundary—she is positioned at the edge of the familiar, facing the unknown
A socioeconomic clue—she is poorer than her classmates
A future setting shift—the story will move into the forest permanently
A literal survival theme—nature will be the central conflict

questions.prompt: Joanna’s ‘brain as an advanced computer’ claim is best read as representing which philosophical stance?
options: Materialism / reductionism (mind explained fully by physical processes)
Dualism (mind and body are separate substances)
Idealism (reality is fundamentally mental)
Skepticism (nothing can be known)

questions.prompt: Sophie’s hesitation about the computer analogy implies a commitment (even if unformed) to:
options: The idea that lived experience (subjectivity) isn’t captured by ‘hardware’ descriptions
The belief that humans have no physical basis at all
The view that robots are morally evil
The belief that school should teach only practical skills

questions.prompt: The chapter title ‘The Garden of Eden’ is most likely doing what, at this early stage?
options: Framing the story as an origin-question narrative: beginnings, innocence, and first questions
Hinting Sophie will study theology instead of philosophy
Indicating a romantic subplot is about to start
Referring to a real park Sophie will visit

questions.prompt: In Chapter 2, Sophie’s frustration with school (‘trivialities’) is best understood as:
options: A clash between institutional learning and existential inquiry
A sign she dislikes learning languages specifically
A plot device to make her skip school later
A complaint that teachers are personally unfair to her

questions.prompt: The quote about philosophy requiring the ‘faculty of wonder’ positions wonder as:
options: The engine that turns the obvious into a problem worth thinking about
A childish phase to outgrow
A synonym for having many opinions
A talent for winning debates

questions.prompt: Sophie’s choice to keep the letters secret (for now) most strongly suggests:
options: She senses the questions are intimate—something to ‘own’ before socializing
She is afraid Joanna will steal her homework
She suspects her father is sending them
She is planning to run away

questions.prompt: The contrast ‘thinking about big questions’ vs ‘memorizing irregular verbs’ is mainly used to highlight:
options: A hierarchy of meaning—skills matter, but they’re not the same as understanding existence
That languages are useless
That Sophie is academically weak
That her teachers are incompetent

questions.prompt: The title ‘The Top Hat’ most plausibly signals the chapter’s theme of:
options: Everyday reality as a kind of stage trick—how the familiar can hide the mysterious
A coming fashion trend at Sophie’s school
A literal character who wears a top hat
A political symbol

questions.prompt: Chapter 3 places a letter from Sophie’s father alongside the mysterious brown envelope. What effect does this pairing create?
options: It heightens suspense by juxtaposing ordinary affection with uncanny inquiry
It resolves the mystery by confirming the father sent the envelopes
It shifts the story away from philosophy into travel writing
It shows Sophie no longer cares about her father

questions.prompt: The chapter’s opening boredom (‘interminable day’) serves which deeper purpose?
options: It makes the arrival of philosophical mystery feel like relief and necessity, not entertainment
It proves Sophie is lazy
It foreshadows that school will be cancelled
It indicates the author dislikes teachers

questions.prompt: The title ‘The Myths’ most naturally connects to Sophie’s situation by suggesting myths are:
options: Early frameworks humans used to explain what later becomes philosophy
Pure lies told to control children
Only religious propaganda
Unrelated to real life—just stories

questions.prompt: The quote about a balance between good and evil most strongly prepares the reader for:
options: Mythic thinking as a way to map moral and cosmic order onto the world
A detective plot with criminals
A coming war scene
A sports rivalry

questions.prompt: Across Chapters 1–3, what is the most consistent ‘trigger’ that moves Sophie from routine life into philosophical attention?
options: Moments where the everyday cracks open—ordinary scenes become questions (mind, world, meaning)
A desire to outperform Joanna academically
A plan to become a scientist
A dislike of her neighborhood
